text:
Public participation in urban planning has been at the focus of urban research for decades, but there seems to be a clear gap between policymakers and ordinary citizens. While the literature on public participation focuses largely on reactionary participation (i.e. participation in projects where planning decisions have already been made by policy makers) it appears that contributive participation, where ordinary citizens in a community try to redefine and revitalise the vacant and derelict parts of the urban landscape in order to suit their needs and visions, is barely researched. Contributive participation in urban renewal implies projects where citizens have ideas for the reuse of particular areas of land and then, with the agreement of official bodies, proceed to physically change the land in accordance with their ideas. Such projects start from a potential new use of the land and end with changes made to the physical landscape.

Local initiatives are complex phenomena which have often been described in terms of the notion of reaction versus contribution to society and have been understood, therefore, in relation to the concept of community. The paper aims to understand the local initiatives of urban regeneration that are carried out through two different paths – as a form of reaction or as a way to contribute. The research is based on two comparative case studies that have enabled the identification of the different triggers and barriers for each type of action. On the basis of the analysis, the activation of contributive participation appears to be a powerful tool for regeneration and it is especially appropriate for post-crisis city spaces since it is possible to act in an optimum way in the moments when the real estate crisis causes a huge number of vacant buildings. The conclusions emphasize the need to adapt and change current spatial planning for a more efficient and less bureaucratic system that takes into account the dynamics of the grass-root regeneration activities and therefore includes ways to channel the energy of society towards a more creative use of the urban space.
